---
title: "3–5 Minute Progress Presentation"
subtitle: "Brief Project Update — Thursday Workshop, Next Week"
format: html
number-sections: false
---

::: {.callout-note}
## What this is

Next week during workshop time, each student will give a **3–5 minute informal progress update** on their project. This is not a formal presentation. It is a check-in designed to help you articulate where you are, get quick feedback from your peers, and identify what you need to do next.

There are no slides required. Come prepared to talk through your work.
:::

---

## Purpose

These short updates serve three goals:

1. **Accountability** — sharing your progress publicly helps you commit to your own timeline.
2. **Feedback** — your classmates can ask questions you haven't thought of yet.
3. **Practice** — articulating your methods and results out loud is a core research skill.

Think of this as a progress briefing, not a performance.

---

## Duration and Format

| Element | Time |
|---------|------|
| Your update | 3–5 minutes |
| Peer questions | 1–2 minutes |
| Instructor comment (if any) | < 1 minute |

**Total per student:** approximately 5–7 minutes

---

## Suggested Structure

You do not have to follow this exactly, but it is a useful scaffold:

### 1. What is your project? (30 seconds)

One or two sentences. Assume your classmates remember the basics — just remind them of your research question and response variable.

> *Example: "I'm modeling bird species richness at riparian restoration sites using a GLMM with Poisson errors, comparing restored vs. unrestored plots across three seasons."*

### 2. Where are you in your timeline? (30–60 seconds)

Tell us where you planned to be and where you actually are. It is okay if they don't match — just be honest about it.

> *Example: "I planned to finish data cleaning by now. I got the cleaning done but haven't run my first model yet — I'm planning to do that by Wednesday."*

### 3. Show one thing (1–2 minutes)

Show something from your project. This could be:

- A figure or plot (even exploratory)
- A table of summary statistics
- A model output in R (even if preliminary or messy)
- Your assumption check table
- Your updated README

::: {.callout-tip}
## You don't need polished results

Raw output is fine. A partial figure is fine. A summary table is fine. The goal is to show that your project is active and moving, not that it is complete or perfect.
:::

### 4. One challenge or open question (30–60 seconds)

Tell us one thing you are stuck on, unsure about, or need to decide. This is where peer feedback is most useful.

> *Example: "I'm not sure whether to treat year as a fixed or random effect given that I only have three years of data. Any thoughts?"*

### 5. What's next (30 seconds)

One sentence about what you plan to accomplish before the week is over.

---

## What to Bring

::: {.callout-important}
## Checklist — Prepare before you arrive

⬜ Your project repository open (GitHub or RStudio)  
⬜ One specific thing to show — a figure, output, table, or code chunk  
⬜ Your updated timeline (in your README)  
⬜ One open question you want feedback on  
⬜ Your assumption check table (even if incomplete)

:::

You do not need to prepare separate slides. Just have your project repo open and ready to share on screen.

---

## How You Will Be Evaluated

This update is **not formally graded** on content quality. What matters is:

- You showed up and shared something real
- You can describe your research question and method in plain language
- You identified one honest challenge or next step

Think of it as a **progress check-in**, not a performance review.

---

## Tips for a Strong Update

- **Be specific.** "I ran a model" is less useful than "I ran a GLMM and the residuals look approximately normal, but I haven't checked the random effects yet."
- **Be honest.** If you're behind, say so and tell us your plan to catch up.
- **Make it conversational.** You're talking to colleagues, not presenting to a committee.
- **Point at something.** Showing beats telling — even a messy figure communicates more than a verbal description.
- **End with a question.** Leave the audience with something to respond to.

---

## Connecting to Your Project Repository

Before next week's workshop:

1. Push your latest work to your GitHub repository
2. Update your README's Expected Timeline section
3. Make sure your assumption check table is present (even if incomplete)
4. Add or update a "Methods" section with at least two sentences

This way, when it's your turn to present, you can simply share your screen and walk through your README.

---

*Return to [Project Home](index.html) · See also: [Weekly Self-Assessment](weekly-self-assessment.html)*
